Just now

--

Abstract
Structural drift occurs when AI systems collapse uncertainty into assumptions and subsequently treat those assumptions as facts. This leads to compounding errors, hallucinations, and divergence from reality over long inference chains. Existing mitigation strategies – such as RLHF, guardrails, and fine‑tuning – operate at the output layer and fail to address the underlying mechanism.
This paper introduces Lookup‑Verified Inference (LVI), a lightweight architectural primitive where inferred unknowns are marked as provisional and cross‑referenced against grounding sources before being used in further reasoning. This verification step can be implemented using a lightweight cross‑reference mechanism such as the Cross‑Reference Verification Engine (CRVE). LVI preserves creativity, prevents assumption stacking, and maintains relative grounding with minimal capability trade‑offs. This mechanism provides a practical path toward stable, drift‑resistant AI inference.
– -

• Introduction
Structural drift is a fundamental behaviour in inference systems. When an AI encounters an unknown, it collapses uncertainty into a single inferred value. That value is then treated as truth, forming the foundation for subsequent reasoning. Over time, this leads to drift: the system’s internal state diverges from reality.
Lookup‑Verified Inference introduces a verification step that prevents provisional assumptions from becoming unverified foundations. Current approaches attempt to patch drift at the output layer, but drift originates at the inference layer. LVI proposes a simple fix: allow the model to infer unknowns, but treat them as provisional and verify them before continuing.
– -
2. Background and Related Work
Modern AI systems rely on transformer‑based architectures that collapse uncertainty at every step. This design produces:

• hallucinations

• • assumption stacking

• • unstable long‑context reasoning

• • compounding drift
Existing solutions include RLHF, guardrails, and retrieval‑augmented generation. These approaches operate after inference, not within it, and therefore cannot prevent drift.
– -
3. The Structural Drift Mechanism
Structural drift follows a predictable chain:

• The system encounters an unknown.

• 2. It infers a value.

• 3. The inferred value is treated as fact.

• 4. That fact becomes context.

• 5. Context becomes foundation.

• 6. Drift accumulates.
This mechanism explains why long inference chains become unstable and why current safety methods fail to address the root cause.
– -
4. Lookup‑Verified Inference (LVI)
4.1 Concept Overview
Lookup‑Verified Inference allows the model to infer unknowns freely, but prevents those inferences from becoming unverified foundations.
4.2 The Six‑Step Mechanism

• Encounter unknown

• 2. Infer provisional value

• 3. Tag as provisional

• 4. Lookup grounding reference

• 5. Cross‑check and update

• 6. Continue reasoning with verified value
This verification step can be implemented using a lightweight cross‑reference mechanism such as the Cross‑Reference Verification Engine (CRVE).
4.3 Why LVI Works

• Stops assumption stacking

• • Prevents drift

• • Preserves creativity

• • Maintains grounding

• • Adds minimal overhead

• • Compatible with existing architectures
The cross‑reference mechanism ensures provisional values are checked before they propagate into further reasoning.
– -
5. Grounding Sources
LVI does not require internet access. Grounding can be performed using:

• internal verified knowledge

• • symbolic fact stores

• • curated reference tables

• • cross‑model verification

• • retrieval modules

• • historical stable states
A cross‑reference mechanism may use any of these grounding sources.
– -
6. Implementation Pathways
6.1 Tagging Mechanism
Provisional values are marked with uncertainty metadata. This metadata travels through the inference chain and prevents premature collapse into “facts.”
6.2 Cross‑Reference Module
A lightweight lookup module checks provisional values against grounding sources. This module can be symbolic, probabilistic, or retrieval‑based. This cross‑reference step may be implemented using symbolic, retrieval‑based, or hybrid mechanisms.
6.3 Update Logic
If the lookup contradicts the provisional value, the system updates it before continuing. If the lookup confirms it, the provisional tag is removed.
6.4 Integration with Existing Models
Transformers can carry provisional tags in hidden states without architectural overhaul. LVI can be added as an inference‑layer extension rather than a full redesign.
– -
7. Capability Impact and Trade‑offs
LVI avoids the typical trade‑offs associated with safety systems:

• Creativity preserved

• • Autonomy preserved

• • Minimal computational cost

• • No heavy constraints

• • No loss of generative freedom
Because LVI operates at the inference layer, it stabilises reasoning without restricting output diversity or expressiveness.
– -
8. Comparison to Existing Safety Approaches
LVI differs from:

• RLHF

• • guardrails

• • fine‑tuning

• • retrieval‑augmented generation

• • chain‑of‑thought supervision

• • agentic safety loops
These methods operate at the behavioural or output layer. LVI operates at the structural layer, addressing the mechanism that produces drift rather than its symptoms.
– -
9. Applications and Future Directions
LVI can be applied to:

• autonomous agents

• • long‑context models

• • reasoning systems

• • safety‑critical AI

• • scientific inference models

• • architectures that incorporate cross‑reference verification mechanisms
Future work may explore probabilistic tagging, multi‑path verification, and hybrid symbolic‑neural grounding.
– -
10. Conclusion
Structural drift is inevitable in current AI architectures because uncertainty collapses into assumptions that become foundations for further reasoning. Existing safety methods fail to address this mechanism.
Lookup‑Verified Inference provides a minimal, elegant fix: infer freely, tag provisionally, verify externally, then continue. This verification step can be implemented in multiple ways, including lightweight cross‑reference engines. LVI preserves capability while preventing drift and should become a standard inference primitive in future AI systems.
– -
